column6: Developmental Dilemmas: The Role of Power and Agency
column13: Grinnell College | UNU-WIDER | Cambridge University Press | Kunal Sen | United Nations University World Institute for Development Economics Research | University of Manchester
column14: Development economics | Economic development | Institutional economics | Power resources | Corruption | Identity politics | Collective behavior | development economics | collective-action problems | power | agency | institutional entrepreneurs | social identities | resource conflict | political settlements | corruption | inclusive development
column16: A scholarly monograph analyzing why developmental policies often fail, focusing on the interplay of power, agency, collective-action problems, and institutional evolution in development economics. Drawing from case studies and theoretical frameworks, the work examines how powerful agents shape institutions, the dynamics of leadership, brokerage, and institutional entrepreneurship, and the emergence of social cleavages and resource conflict. The text synthesizes literature on collective action, new institutionalism, social conflict, political settlements, and identity politics to provide a conceptual foundation for developmental policy analysis.

column16: Supplementary material providing detailed appendices for the work 'Developmental Dilemmas: The Role of Power and Agency.' The document includes theoretical models, typologies, and game-theoretic frameworks related to political settlements, leader-follower dynamics, the influence of institutional entrepreneurs on social norms, resource conflict, and collective-action problems. It features mathematical models, diagrams, references, and explanatory notes, supporting the main text's exploration of power, agency, and institutional development in economic and political contexts.

column6: Spaulding Model H Touring Car Announcement
column13: Spaulding Mfg. Co. | Grinnell, Iowa | Gray and Davis | The Automobile
column14: Automobiles | Touring cars (Racing automobiles) | Spaulding Model H | automobile design | touring car | 1914 automobiles | sleeping car | Grinnell Iowa | Spaulding Mfg. Co.
column16: Magazine excerpt announcing the Spaulding Model H touring car, previously known as Model G, highlighting its radical body refinements and mechanical changes for 1914. The article details the car's transformation capability into a sleeping car, the addition of an electric reading lamp, increased interior space, streamlined design, and revised fuel tank arrangement. Technical specifications such as the four-cylinder motor, three-point motor suspension, and seating arrangement for five passengers are included. Published by Spaulding Mfg. Co., Grinnell, Iowa, and originally appearing in The Automobile magazine.

column14: Oral history | World War, 1939-1945 | Community life | Family relationships | Education | Women's rights | Glove industry | oral history | Grinnell | Great Depression | World War II | women's history | education | community life | local industry | family life | social clubs | Lannom family | Morrison-Shults glove factory | DeLong company | Worth Baseballs | Grinnell College | Friends of Stewart Library
column16: A transcript of an oral history interview conducted by Dolores Smith with Dorothy Lannom for the Friends of Stewart Library Oral History Project. The interview covers Dorothy Lannom's experiences in Grinnell, Iowa, during the 1930s and 1940s, including her early life, teaching career, marriage, family life, community involvement, local industry, social clubs, and the impact of the Great Depression and World War II on local society. The conversation also discusses the Lannom family's role in local manufacturing, changes in women's roles, local traditions, and notable community members.

column14: Correspondence | Military correspondence | Air mail envelopes | World War, 1939-1945 | Letter writing | World War II | air mail | family letter | 477th Bombardment Squad | Barksdale Field | Lakota Iowa | return to sender | military correspondence
column16: Handwritten letter from H. R. Ley in Lakota, Iowa, addressed to Staff Sgt James J. Ley of the 477th Bombardment Squad at Barksdale Field, Louisiana. The letter discusses family news, home activities such as painting porch furniture and preparing for a bridge club lunch, and conveys affection and well-wishes. The envelope bears multiple postal markings, including air mail and return-to-sender stamps, and was ultimately unclaimed and returned to the sender.
